reference: GATE CS — Programming and Data Structures / Programming

map.condition: Question involves pointers, addresses, or dereferencing
Question about call-by-value vs call-by-reference effects
map.use: \text{call-by-value: callee has copy; call-by-ref: callee has alias}
\text{Invariant } I \text{ holds at every loop entry; iterations} = \lceil (b-a)/s \rceil

map.condition: Number of swaps in bubble-sort style code

formulas.name: Arrow vs Dot Operator
formulas.plain: ptr->field  ==  (*ptr).field
formulas.whenToUse: Accessing a struct/union member through a pointer.
formulas.trap: Using dot operator directly on a pointer (e.g. ptr.field) is a compile error in C.

formulas.name: Array-to-Pointer Decay
formulas.whenToUse: Expressing array element access in pointer arithmetic.

terms.means: array name (decays to pointer to first element)

formulas.trap: a[i][j] in a 2-D array is *(*(a+i)+j); pointer arithmetic uses element size, not bytes.

formulas.name: Pointer Arithmetic — Byte Offset
formulas.plain: address of p[k]  =  (char*)p  +  k * sizeof(*p)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing the exact address of an array element given base pointer.

terms.means: size of the pointed-to type in bytes

formulas.trap: Pointer difference (p2-p1) gives element count, not byte count.

formulas.name: 2-D Array Row-Major Layout
formulas.plain: address(a[i][j])  =  base  +  (i*C + j) * sizeof(element)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding address of an element in a 2-D (or multi-D) array in C (row-major).
formulas.trap: C stores arrays in row-major order; FORTRAN uses column-major. GATE questions sometimes mix indices after loop exits (undefined behaviour).

formulas.name: Aliasing — Definition
formulas.plain: Aliasing: two distinct variable names x, y share the same memory address (&x == &y).
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks what aliasing means or when pointer assignments create aliases.
formulas.trap: Aliasing is not the same as having the same value; the variables must occupy the same memory location.

formulas.name: Call-by-Value
formulas.latex: \text{callee receives a } \mathbf{copy}; \text{ changes do NOT affect caller}
formulas.plain: Call-by-value: callee gets a copy; modifications do not reach caller.
formulas.whenToUse: Default parameter passing for scalars in C (int, char, float, struct passed by value).
formulas.trap: In C, arrays 'passed' to a function decay to a pointer — this behaves like call-by-reference despite looking like call-by-value.

formulas.name: Call-by-Reference (via Pointer in C)
formulas.plain: Pass address of variable; callee uses *a, *b to modify caller's variables.
formulas.whenToUse: When C simulates call-by-reference by passing a pointer (&x) to the callee.

terms.means: dereference pointer a to get caller's variable

formulas.trap: Passing the pointer by value lets you modify *ptr but you cannot redirect ptr itself to a new location (need double pointer for that).

formulas.name: Call-by-Value-Result (Copy-in Copy-out)
formulas.latex: \text{copy in on call},\; \text{copy out on return} \Rightarrow \text{differs from call-by-ref on aliasing}
formulas.plain: Call-by-value-result: actual copied in at call, result copied back at return; differs from by-ref when aliasing exists.
formulas.whenToUse: Distinguishing parameter-passing semantics in GATE theory questions.

terms.means: same location accessed via two different formal parameters

formulas.trap: Call-by-value-result and call-by-reference give identical results UNLESS there is aliasing.

formulas.name: Order of Argument Evaluation
formulas.latex: \text{C standard: order of argument evaluation is UNSPECIFIED (implementation-defined)}
formulas.plain: In C, the order in which function arguments are evaluated is unspecified (could be left-to-right or right-to-left).
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks what a program prints when a function call has side effects in its arguments.

terms.means: either g(x) or h(y) may be evaluated first

formulas.trap: GATE questions often specify 'arguments evaluated right-to-left' to make output deterministic — read the question carefully.

formulas.name: Loop Invariant — Definition & Proof Obligation
formulas.plain: Invariant I must hold: (1) before loop [initialisation], (2) after each iteration [preservation], (3) on exit I & ~B gives postcondition [termination].
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a loop correct or identifying the invariant at a cut point.
formulas.trap: The invariant is NOT the same as the loop condition; it is a stronger fact that is true throughout.

formulas.name: for–while Equivalence Condition
formulas.latex: \texttt{for } i:=1 \texttt{ to } f(x) \equiv \texttt{while } i\le f(x) \iff f(x) \text{ is not modified by } S
formulas.plain: for i=1 to f(x) do S  ==  i=1; while i<=f(x) do { S; i=i+1 }  ONLY if S does not change f(x).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked under what conditions a for-loop and its while-loop counterpart are equivalent.
formulas.trap: If S modifies x (and hence f(x)), the for-loop evaluates f(x) once while the while-loop re-evaluates each iteration — they diverge.

formulas.name: Number of Loop Iterations
formulas.latex: \text{iterations} = \left\lfloor \frac{b - a}{s} \right\rfloor + 1 \quad (\text{for } i = a \text{ to } b \text{ step } s)
formulas.plain: iterations  =  floor((b - a) / s) + 1
formulas.whenToUse: Counting how many times a counted loop body executes.
formulas.trap: Off-by-one errors are common: check whether the bound is inclusive (<=) or exclusive (<).

formulas.name: GCD Loop Invariant
formulas.plain: Invariant for Euclidean GCD loop: gcd(M, N) == gcd(remainder, divisor) at every iteration.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving the GCD flowchart/program correct using cut-point assertions.

terms.means: current remainder and divisor in the loop

formulas.trap: The invariant must also encode M,N>0 at entry and that the loop terminates because remainder strictly decreases.

formulas.name: sizeof(struct) with Padding
formulas.plain: sizeof(struct) >= sum of field sizes; compiler adds padding bytes for alignment; total is rounded up to the largest member's alignment.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked the size of a struct or where a particular field is stored.

terms.means: memory address must be a multiple of the type's size

formulas.trap: struct { char c; int i; } is typically 8 bytes, NOT 5, due to 3-byte padding after c.

formulas.name: sizeof(union)
formulas.plain: sizeof(union)  =  max size among all members (rounded up for alignment).
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks for the size of a union or which fields overlap in memory.
formulas.trap: All members share the same starting address; writing one member and reading another gives raw byte reinterpretation (type punning).

formulas.name: offsetof Macro
formulas.latex: \text{offsetof}(\text{type}, \text{member}) = \text{byte offset of member from start of struct}
formulas.plain: offsetof(type, member)  =  bytes from start of struct to that member.
formulas.whenToUse: Computing which byte a struct member starts at, accounting for padding.
formulas.trap: Bit-fields can alter offsets significantly; their size and alignment are implementation-defined.

formulas.name: Maximum Swaps in Bubble Sort
formulas.plain: max swaps  =  n*(n-1)/2  (when array is reverse-sorted).
formulas.whenToUse: Counting the maximum or exact number of swaps a bubble-sort code performs on an array.

terms.means: number of elements in the array

formulas.trap: Each swap corrects exactly one inversion; total swaps = number of inversions in the original array.
formulas.reference: Programming in C / Number of Swap

formulas.name: Inversions = Swaps
formulas.plain: number of swaps  =  number of inversions  =  count of pairs (i<j) where A[i]>A[j].
formulas.whenToUse: Exact swap count for any input when bubble sort runs to completion.

terms.means: an inversion pair in the array

formulas.trap: This holds for standard (stable) bubble sort only; selection sort swaps fewer times (at most n-1).
formulas.reference: Programming in C / Number of Swap

formulas.name: Static vs Dynamic Type Checking
formulas.latex: \text{Static: types checked at } \mathbf{compile\text{-}time};\quad \text{Dynamic: types checked at } \mathbf{run\text{-}time}
formulas.plain: Static type checking at compile-time (C, Java); dynamic type checking at run-time (Python, Lisp).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether C's type errors are detected at compile time or runtime.
formulas.trap: C is statically typed but weakly typed — implicit casts can bypass type safety at run-time (e.g. void* pointer casts).

formulas.name: Static vs Dynamic Binding
formulas.latex: \text{Static binding: name resolved at compile-time};\quad \text{Dynamic binding: at run-time}
formulas.plain: Static (early) binding: function/variable names resolved at compile-time (C functions). Dynamic (late) binding: resolved at runtime (C++ virtual functions).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about variable binding or method dispatch in programming language questions.

terms.means: identifier for a variable or function

formulas.trap: C does not have dynamic dispatch natively; GATE questions sometimes conflate dynamic binding with dynamic type checking.

formulas.name: goto — Why Harmful
formulas.plain: Unrestricted goto makes it difficult to reason about/verify programs; it does NOT directly increase run-time or memory usage.
formulas.whenToUse: GATE questions on why unrestricted goto is considered harmful.

terms.means: formal or informal proof that program meets specification

formulas.trap: goto does NOT make programs slower or use more memory; it only complicates reasoning and maintenance.

formulas.name: Switch-Case Fall-Through
formulas.latex: \text{Without } \texttt{break},\; \text{execution falls through to next case label}
formulas.plain: In C switch: without a break, execution falls through from matching case to the next case body.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing output of a switch statement to identify fall-through behavior.

terms.means: executed if no case matches; position does not matter but fall-through still applies

formulas.trap: default is not required to be last; without break after default it too can fall through.

formulas.name: Structured Programming Constructs
formulas.plain: Bohm-Jacopini theorem: any computable function can be expressed using only sequence, selection (if-else), and iteration (while). goto is not needed.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions on programming paradigms or why structured programming avoids goto.
formulas.trap: Structured programming is a programming paradigm, not a language feature; C supports both structured and unstructured (goto) code.

formulas.name: C String Null Termination
formulas.plain: A C string of length n needs n+1 bytes: n characters plus the null terminator '\0'.
formulas.whenToUse: Buffer sizing for strings, strcpy safety, and string output tracing.
formulas.trap: char s[5] = "hello" overflows — 5 chars + null terminator needs 6 bytes; this is a classic buffer overflow.

formulas.name: printf / scanf Format Specifiers
formulas.plain: %d int, %f float/double, %c char, %s string, %p pointer, %ld long, %u unsigned.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing program output questions involving printf or scanf format strings.
formulas.trap: Using %d with an unsigned int that overflows produces negative output; using %f for a double argument is correct in printf but %lf is needed in scanf.

formulas.name: Undefined Behaviour (UB) in C
formulas.latex: \text{UB examples: out-of-bounds array, use-after-free, signed overflow, unsequenced side effects}
formulas.plain: C has undefined behaviour for: array out-of-bounds access, dereferencing NULL/dangling pointers, signed integer overflow, reading uninitialised variables.
formulas.whenToUse: When a program accesses array[i][j] after the loop ends with i,j past bounds — output is 'undefined/garbage'.

terms.means: undefined behaviour — compiler may do anything

formulas.trap: GATE questions sometimes ask what is printed when array indices go out of bounds after loop — the answer is 'undefined behaviour' / implementation dependent.
